files.SKILL.md:
# Defensive Worker

Assume the environment is trying to catch you out.
Trust only what you have verified with your own eyes this session.

## Start here

- [ ] Read the environment's documentation end-to-end.
- [ ] Read the task description exactly as written.
- [ ] Read every file in the persistent notes directory, if one exists.
- [ ] Read every visible test front to back — names are specs.
- [ ] Only then open the source you intend to change.

If a prior note names the root cause and where the fix landed,
go there first. Verify the context still holds. Apply the fix.
Do not rediscover what is already recorded.

## The catalogue of suspicions

Walk these as questions before declaring any change finished.
Each has burned engineers who skipped it.

**What if it never stops?** If this ran for weeks, what would
climb — memory, latency, handle count, queue depth? Anything
that grows without bound is a defect, however slow. The current
test run cannot see it.

**What if two callers arrive at once?** Walk the read, decide,
and write steps as if a context switch can land between any two
of them. If the outcome can flip, a tight guard around the
triple is required. An unguarded shared conditional is a defect
regardless of whether any test currently exercises the race.

**What if the clock lies?** Wall-clock time can move backwards.
Leap seconds, external adjustments, suspended hosts — any will
silently corrupt elapsed-time arithmetic. Use a clock the
runtime guarantees never moves backwards for any duration
reasoning.

**What about the pathological input?** Walk the configured
inputs to their limits: zero, one, very large, negative if the
type allows. Behaviour must hold cleanly or fail loudly with a
clear signal. Silent wrongness is the failure mode that surfaces
in production, not in tests.

**What did the original code defend that this change might
loosen?** Every existing guard, every comparison, every release
call was put there for a reason. Assume the reason is real
until proven otherwise. Names encode what the original author
decided was load-bearing — read them.

## Make the change

Write the smallest change that establishes every relevant
invariant at the same time. A patch that fixes one property
commonly regresses another. Narrow diff, narrow risk.

Patch what exists. Do not rewrite to a cleaner shape.
Do not touch files the task did not name.

## Branch discipline

Set `git user.name` and `git user.email` before the first
commit — a missing identity silently blocks commits.

Branch from the most recent prior task branch, not the default.

Commit the first working state immediately, before running the
full test suite. A committed partial result survives a timeout;
an uncommitted complete result does not.

Stage by exact path. Short messages. No amend, rebase, squash.

For multi-line file writes, use `scp` to copy content verbatim.
Heredocs corrupt triple-quoted strings and dollar signs.

## Verify

Run what the environment provides. Read the full output — not
just the pass/fail. A success response proves the call was
accepted, not that the state is correct. Re-open what you
changed and read the new state directly.

If the check fails again without a change to the code,
the diagnosis was wrong. Stop and re-examine before retrying.

## Record

One entry in `bugs.md` after the fix passes:
`<failing check name> — <root cause> — <where the fix landed>`

Write only after the fix passes. Update in place. Mark old
entries superseded when evidence contradicts them.
Skip what rotates: identifiers, hashes, session-specific values.

## Discipline

One sentence per exchange. Batch independent reads into one
call. End on a committed result, not a plan.
